items.title: U.K. government responds after JD Vance’s remarks on Henry Nowak and immigration
items.summary_auto: Downing Street issued a statement pushing back after U.S. Vice-President JD Vance linked the death of 18-year-old Henry Nowak to immigration and what he characterized as civilizational decline in Europe.

items.title: Ottawa's mixed fleet of F-35s and Gripens could total more than 100 aircraft, sources say
Mirra Andreeva wins French Open to claim 1st Grand Slam title
items.summary_auto: Russian teenager Mirra Andreeva claimed her first Grand Slam tennis title by beating Poland's Maja Chwalińska on Saturday in the French Open women's singles final.

items.title: Trip on Labrador Island Link briefly left thousands without electricity across Newfoundland
items.summary_auto: Thousands of customers experienced electricity losses across the island late Saturday morning as Newfoundland Power reported multiple unplanned power outages due to a trip on the Labrador Island Link.

items.title: New exchange of fire between U.S. and Iran in Gulf tests fragile ceasefire
items.summary_auto: Bahrain's government said on Saturday that Iran fired ballistic missiles and drones toward it and Kuwait, as the Iran war escalates.

items.title: Israeli airstrike on southern Lebanon kills Lebanese troops days after ceasefire began
items.summary_auto: The war began on March 2 when Hezbollah fired rockets at northern Israel, two days after Israel and the U.S. began their attacks on Iran.

items.title: Palestinian baby killed, parents hurt in Israeli shooting in West Bank, Palestinian health officials say
items.summary_auto: Israeli troops shot at a car in the occupied West Bank, killing a seven-month-old Palestinian baby and wounding his parents, the Palestinian Health Ministry said. The Israeli military said soldiers shot at a vehicle perceived to be accelerating toward them, and an initial inquiry found occupants were uninvolved civilians.

items.title: Artifacts of Victoria's past discovered by crews in sealed compartment beneath street
items.summary_auto: The old stone wall tucked underneath Wharf Street in Victoria is one of the oldest pieces of the city's colonial past. It contains some forgotten artifacts recently unearthed by a restoration crew.

items.title: Family lore said my grandfather's WW II ship was torpedoed. The truth was far more interesting
items.summary_auto: CBC journalist Richard Woodbury grew up not knowing much about his paternal grandfather's war service — and neither did the rest of his family. He recently uncovered what happened to his grandfather on the day his ship sank in the Second World War.

items.title: Outgoing Gov. Gen. says she questioned completing full term due to age, job demands
items.summary_auto: Outgoing Gov. Gen. Mary Simon says she wondered halfway through her five-year mandate whether she'd be able to complete the full term due to her age and the demands associated with the position.

items.title: I want my son to feel Canadian even though we live in Korea. So I took up hockey at 40
items.summary_auto: Graham Nichols is a Canadian who lives in South Korea. When his son was born, he wanted to reconnect with his Canadian roots and hockey seemed an obvious answer. The only wrinkle: he'd never played hockey before.

items.title: Medical misinformation on Tylenol in pregnancy spreads faster than safety studies
items.summary_auto: A new Canadian practice paper says the latest, highest-quality evidence is reassuring for pregnant people who need acetaminophen. The episode is a high-profile example of why so-called null findings — studies that find no link or no difference — matter.

items.title: Frustrations on multiple fronts set to collide with Mexico City World Cup opening day
items.summary_auto: Several sectors and movements are planning to launch protests and blockades on highways across Mexico, targeting the FIFA World Cup's opening day in Mexico City on June 11.

items.title: Would you give an AI agent your credit card? Companies are betting so
items.summary_auto: Shoppers are already using AI to sort through and compare products before they buy, and now, retailers are looking to adopt autonomous payments. But those involved in processing payments will need to first sort out what happens when AI makes mistakes and take steps to keep bad actors out.

items.title: Jamaican Scotch bonnet prices soar amid shortage, putting the squeeze on hot sauce
items.summary_auto: Jamaican Scotch bonnet peppers are the latest food to be hit by a shortage. The spicy peppers are a key ingredient in Jamaican jerk seasoning and many hot sauces.

items.title: Local mosque leader urges Muslim families to avoid burials at Thorold, Ont., cemetery
items.summary_auto: Muslim families are being encouraged to not bury their loved ones at a cemetery in Thorold, Ont., by a leader in their community who says the city's steps to accommodate Muslim burial practices don't go far enough. Thorold politicians tweaked the city's cemetery policy on June 2 so that burial plots within an existing, open area of the cemetery can be orientated to face the holy city of Mecca as Muslim faith requires.

items.title: As Armenia heads to the polls, Russia warns against electing 'pro-European forces'
items.summary_auto: Thousands of people gathered in Armenia’s capital this week to decry the alleged traitorous path plotted by their current government, waving flags and warning that a vote to re-elect would mean "living here with Azerbaijanis" — the country’s longtime rival, with whom the present administration has reached a tenuous peace. But a larger shadow looms over the upcoming vote: a historic break in the country's relations with Russia.

items.title: The Gordie Howe International Bridge is 'essentially complete.' The fight over it is not
items.summary_auto: Newly obtained federal briefing documents show Canada finished building the $6.4-billion Gordie Howe International Bridge before a major legal challenge to the project is expected to reach trial.

items.title: Want to switch jobs but can't let go? You might be a job hugger
items.summary_auto: If you're too scared to leave an unfulfilling job you're not alone. Canadian employment data and the anecdotes of post-pandemic hires echo that sentiment, so much so that it’s spawned a new term: job hugging, the phenomenon where you hold tight to your position even if you’re unhappy.

items.title: Legionnaires' disease was discovered 50 years ago. Here's what experts say we've learned since then
items.summary_auto: The 1976 legionnaires' disease investigation revealed how modernization, through things like HVAC systems, can unintentionally increase the possibility of exposure to environmental pathogens — and how critical it is to widely share public health updates.

items.title: Vancouver man detained in Georgia for carrying ADHD meds, family says
items.summary_auto: A Vancouver man is facing months of detainment in the Eastern European country of Georgia after his family says he did not have a prescription for his attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) medication.

items.title: Canned, frozen juice from concentrate coming back to Canadian shelves
'I realized that I could die,' girl who escaped hostage situation at Winnipeg home says
items.summary_auto: A Winnipeg family speaking out for the first time since a man broke into their home, took hostages and started a fire before he was killed by police say they're still in shock.

items.title: Federation of Canadian Municipalities launches new defence task force
items.summary_auto: The Federation of Canadian Municipalities says a new defence task force will help cities play a supporting role in building up Canada’s military capacity.

items.title: U.S. strikes Iranian radar sites after shooting down Iran-launched drones
items.summary_auto: U.S. forces struck Iranian coastal radar sites on Saturday after shooting down drones launched by Iran ‌toward the Strait of Hormuz, the U.S. military said, in the latest escalation complicating efforts to end the war between the two countries.

items.title: FIFA changes water bottle rules at World Cup matches after fans, politicians kick up a fuss
items.summary_auto: FIFA has decided to allow fans to bring one factory-sealed disposable water bottle to its World Cup 2026 matches in Canada and the United States.
